auditMetadata.summary: Security audit identified 14 findings across cryptography, authentication, dependencies, and configuration. Two critical issues require immediate remediation (weak RNG for security-sensitive codes). Frontend token storage in localStorage requires mitigation planning. Multiple high-severity dependency vulnerabilities in axios require upgrade to v1.7.7+ (latest).

criticalFindings.title: Weak RNG for Account Deletion Code Generation
criticalFindings.description: The 6-digit account deletion verification code is generated using Math.random(), which is cryptographically insecure and predictable. An attacker can predict or brute-force this code (1 in 1,000,000 attempts) to delete other users' accounts.

impact.userImpact: Account takeover and deletion of user data. No rate limiting on code verification (criticalBucket is applied at request level, not code attempt level)

criticalFindings.riskScenario: Attacker A crafts 100 parallel requests with codes 000000-000099 to User B's deletion endpoint. With 6-digit brute force space and no per-code attempt limiting, successful deletion is highly probable.

steps: Replace Math.random() with crypto.randomBytes(3) to generate 3 random bytes (24 bits = ~16M combinations)
Encode as hex or base32 for user-friendly format
Implement per-code rate limiting (max 5 attempts per code, 15-minute lockout after failures)
Store code hash (SHA-256) in database instead of plaintext in memory
Add telemetry for failed deletion attempts (fraud signal)

references: CWE-338: Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)

criticalFindings.title: Weak Couple secretKey Generation Using Math.random()
criticalFindings.description: Couple secretKey is generated by concatenating timestamp + Math.random().toString(36). This is predictable because: (1) timestamp is known/guessable, (2) Math.random() entropy ~32 bits, (3) base36 encoding adds no entropy. Total entropy ~40 bits (brute-forceable in seconds).

impact.userImpact: Attacker can generate valid secretKeys for any couple based on timestamp, enabling: (1) unauthorized couple linking, (2) data exfiltration of partner's events/tasks, (3) financial fraud (manipulating point distribution)

criticalFindings.riskScenario: Attacker observes Couple.createdAt timestamp (2026-06-11T14:32:45Z). Within 1 second, they generate ~1000 secretKey candidates and test against /api/couple-preview endpoint. One matches, granting access to couple data without invitation.

steps: Replace with crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') for 256-bit entropy (same pattern already used in createInvitation token)
Remove timestamp; it adds no value and reduces entropy
Update both acceptEmailInvitation (line 56) and acceptProposal (line 159) functions
Audit all existing couples: rotate secretKey for any generated <2026-05-06
Consider secretKey immutable after creation (no rotation needed if strong initially)

references: CWE-338: Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values

highFindings.title: Multiple High-Severity Vulnerabilities in axios Dependency
highFindings.description: Axios <1.7.7 contains 8 identified CVEs affecting proxy handling, DoS resistance, and HTTPS security. Primary impact: credential leakage across HTTP→HTTPS redirects, prototype pollution attacks enabling MITM, and ReDoS via cookie name injection.

impact.userImpact: In production with proxy infrastructure: JWT and refresh tokens could leak to attacker-controlled redirect targets. Prototype pollution enables cache poisoning of requests.

highFindings.affectedContext: Frontend axios usage for all API calls (src/frontend/src/services/api/); backend indirectly via build chain

steps: Update axios to >=1.7.7 in both root package.json and src/frontend/package.json
Run npm audit after upgrade to verify all CVEs are resolved
Test OAuth redirect flows (Google Calendar integration) to confirm HTTPS handling unchanged
Review any proxy configuration in production environment (Render, nginx, etc.) to ensure legitimate use cases still work

highFindings.title: localStorage Stores Authentication Tokens (XSS Vulnerability Surface)
highFindings.description: Both access_token and refresh_token are stored in localStorage (lines 28, 36, 62). While this enables persistent authentication, it exposes tokens to XSS attacks via document.cookie/localStorage access from injected scripts. An XSS vulnerability anywhere in the app grants attacker access to both tokens.

impact.userImpact: If XSS occurs (e.g., via user-supplied text in event names, profiles, journal entries), attacker gains valid JWT + refresh token to impersonate user, access couple data, modify events, delete account.

highFindings.mitigationCurrently: Frontend does implement XSS defenses: React auto-escapes JSX, Zod validation on user input. However, risk exists if: (1) dependencies have XSS exploits, (2) dangerouslySetInnerHTML used without sanitization, (3) server-side HTML injection in email templates (though less critical for tokens).
highFindings.bestPracticesGap: Tokens should ideally be stored in memory or httpOnly cookies. localStorage is explicitly advised against by OWASP for sensitive tokens.

steps: PHASE 1 (Immediate): Document localStorage token storage as known risk in SECURITY.md. Audit all user input surfaces for XSS (event names, profile bios, journal entries).
PHASE 2 (Sprint 27+): Evaluate httpOnly cookie approach (requires backend to set Set-Cookie headers, React Router to handle cookie-based auth flow).
PHASE 3 (Long-term): Consider sessionStorage as interim: harder to exfiltrate but still at risk; only slightly better than localStorage.
PHASE 4: Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) header to restrict script injection vectors.
Add XSS sanitization library (DOMPurify) for any dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage
Enable browser's 'Secure' flag on tokens (HTTPS only)
remediation.shortTermWorkaround: Add event listener to detect XSS attempts: monitor for rapid localStorage access anomalies via service worker

references: OWASP: Authentication Cheat Sheet - Token Storage

highFindings.title: Missing Authorization Check Pattern (Potential Couple-Level IDOR)

files.description: Profile routes use authMiddleware which injects req.coupleId, but not all endpoints validate that the coupleId in the request body matches req.coupleId
Family routes (children, pets) may not check authorization against couple scope

highFindings.description: While authMiddleware correctly sets req.coupleId via JWT verification, some routes may accept explicit coupleId in request bodies without re-validating. This enables IDOR: User A can modify User B's couple data by passing User B's coupleId in a request.

impact.userImpact: User A (Alice) can modify Bob's couple profile (homeType, cohabitation, children, pets) by crafting requests with Bob's coupleId. No detection of who initiated the change.

highFindings.auditedBy: See negotiationIdorContract.test.ts for an example of IDOR testing pattern that should apply to all couple-scoped endpoints

steps: Add authorization check pattern: `if (req.coupleId !== parsedBody.coupleId) return 403` to all couple-scoped endpoints
Refactor to never accept coupleId from request body; always use req.coupleId from JWT
Audit all 36 routes for this pattern
Add contract tests (similar to negotiationIdorContract.test.ts) for each family.ts and profile.ts endpoint
Enable enforceInput middleware that strips coupleId from request body before processing
remediation.codePattern: const coupleId = requireAuth(req).coupleId; // Never accept from body

references: OWASP Top 10 A01:2021 - Broken Access Control
CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

moderateFindings.title: Weak RNG in Email Service Jitter Calculation (Low Impact but Anti-Pattern)

files.snippet: function jitter(ms: number): number { return Math.round(ms * (0.75 + Math.random() * 0.5)) }

moderateFindings.description: Email retry exponential backoff uses Math.random() for jitter. While low-impact (jitter is not security-sensitive), it propagates a weak-RNG anti-pattern throughout the codebase.

impact.userImpact: In rare cases, if all 3 email retry attempts collide on the same millisecond delay, there's a marginally increased risk of thundering herd against Resend API. Negligible in practice.

steps: For consistency and best practices, replace Math.random() with crypto.randomBytes(1)[0] / 255 for 0-1 range
This is a 'nice-to-have' after S0 and S1 critical issues are fixed
remediation.codeExample: function jitter(ms: number): number { const rand = crypto.randomBytes(1)[0] / 255; return Math.round(ms * (0.75 + rand * 0.5)) }

references: CWE-338: Use of Cryptographically Weak PRNG

moderateFindings.title: Hardcoded Test Credentials in Seed Data File
moderateFindings.description: Test credentials (password123, test-secret-123) are hardcoded in git-tracked seed scripts and test helpers. While these are development-only, committed test credentials in public repos are a reconnaissance aid for attackers.

impact.userImpact: If repo is ever public or credentials leak, attackers know exact dev password format, enabling faster testing of backdoors or compromised test accounts.

moderateFindings.remediationNotes: Credentials are not in .gitignore; they are committed to main. This is acceptable for dev-only files, but should be documented.

steps: Move TEST_PASSWORD to src/backend/tests/.env.test (add to .gitignore)
Update test imports to load from env instead of hardcoded export
Update seed-data.ts to use env variable or random password generation
Document in CONTRIBUTING.md that no credentials should be hardcoded

moderateFindings.title: Moderate Severity Vulnerabilities in @typescript-eslint Packages via minimatch
moderateFindings.description: @typescript-eslint packages (v6+) depend on minimatch with a ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) vulnerability in glob pattern matching.

impact.userImpact: Only affects development build time. If an attacker commits a .eslintrc with malicious glob patterns, ESLint could hang during build (unlikely in practice due to code review).

steps: Update @typescript-eslint packages to versions with patched minimatch (v8.0.0+)
Update minimatch directly to >=3.1.0
Run npm audit to verify resolution

moderateFindings.title: Moderate Vulnerabilities in Body-Parser, Express, and express-rate-limit via 'qs' Package
moderateFindings.description: The 'qs' package (used for query string parsing in express/body-parser) has a prototype pollution vulnerability in v6.10.0 - v6.13.0 and v7.0.0 - v7.2.2.

impact.userImpact: Malicious query strings could pollute Object prototype, affecting request handling. Low likelihood in practice due to Zod validation on all endpoints.

steps: Update express to >=4.22.2 (or >=5.0.2 if moving to v5)
Update body-parser to >=1.20.5
Update express-rate-limit to >=8.5.1
Verify qs dependency is patched as transitive dependency

lowFindings.title: Multiple React Router Versions with Low-Severity XSS via 'useLocation'

files.impact: Only affects edge cases where useLocation().search is rendered without sanitization (React auto-escapes in JSX)

lowFindings.description: react-router and react-router-dom have a low-severity XSS in path handling under specific conditions. Impact is minimal due to React's JSX escaping.

steps: Upgrade react-router and react-router-dom to latest (v6.28+)
Verify no dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage with location data

lowFindings.title: WebSocket (ws) Package DOS Vulnerability in Development

files.context: Used by Vitest/dev tools, not in production code

lowFindings.description: The 'ws' package has a low-severity DoS vulnerability affecting WebSocket connections with oversized frames. Only relevant during development; production does not use WebSockets.

steps: Update ws to >=8.15.0
This is a dev dependency, so no production impact

informationalFindings.title: Vite and Vitest Development Tool Vulnerabilities (Low Risk)
informationalFindings.description: Vite and Vitest have identified vulnerabilities in their toolchain (via protobufjs, esbuild transitive dependencies). These only affect build time, not production.

steps: Update vite to >=5.4.6
Update vitest to >=2.1.8
These are low priority as they do not affect runtime security

informationalFindings.title: @vitest/ui Critical Vulnerability (Development Only)
informationalFindings.description: @vitest/ui has a known critical vulnerability in its dependencies. However, this package is only used during development for test UI visualization and does not ship to production.

steps: Update @vitest/ui to the latest version
Consider removing if not actively used (prefer --ui flag or browser output)

informationalFindings.title: Prototype Pollution and IP Address Validation Issues (Moderate Cascading)
informationalFindings.description: Multiple transitive dependencies (ip-address, protobufjs, qs) have prototype pollution or validation issues that cascade through the dependency tree.

steps: Run npm audit --audit-level=high to identify all moderate+ issues
Prioritize fixes by CVSS score and attack vector (network-accessible vs dev-only)

appliedCorrectly: JWT-based stateless authentication with refresh token rotation
Zod schema validation on all API inputs (prevents type confusion attacks)
Password hashing with bcrypt (proper KDF usage)
HTTPS enforced in production (Render + Supabase SSL)
GDPR data export endpoint (/api/account/export) for transparency
Rate limiting on sensitive endpoints (criticalBucket for delete, register, login)
Couple-level data isolation (coupleId in auth middleware)
Refresh token rotation (prevents token reuse attacks)
Separation of access + refresh token storage attempts
gapsRequiringImprovement: Cryptographic randomness not used for secrets (S0-1, S0-2)
localStorage token storage increases XSS attack surface (S1-2)
Missing IDOR checks on some couple-scoped endpoints (S1-3)
Hardcoded test credentials in committed files (S2-2)
Multiple high-severity dependency vulnerabilities unpatched (S1-1, S2-3, S2-4)

immediate.issue: S0-1 - Weak RNG for deletion code

immediate.urgency: CRITICAL - Enables couple IDOR and data theft

highPriority.issue: S1-3 - Add IDOR checks to family/profile routes

mediumPriority.issue: S1-2 - Migrate tokens from localStorage (evaluation phase)

complianceNotes.gdpr: COMPLIANT - Account export (/api/account/export) and deletion (/api/account/delete) endpoints provide Art. 20 (data portability) and Art. 17 (right to be forgotten). Soft-delete ensures no data recovery without audit.
complianceNotes.pci_dss: NOT APPLICABLE - Application does not handle payment cards (Stripe-eligible but not integrated yet per roadmap v3.0).

owasp_top_10_2021.A01_Broken_Access_Control: PARTIAL - Good JWT isolation, but missing couple-scoped IDOR checks (S1-3)
owasp_top_10_2021.A02_Cryptographic_Failures: CRITICAL GAPS - Weak RNG for secrets (S0-1, S0-2), localStorage tokens (S1-2)
owasp_top_10_2021.A03_Injection: COMPLIANT - Zod validation on all inputs, React JSX escaping, HTML escape in email (S2-1 fixed in audit)
owasp_top_10_2021.A04_Insecure_Design: COMPLIANT - Threat modeling evident in refresh token rotation, rate limiting, soft-delete
owasp_top_10_2021.A05_Security_Misconfiguration: MOSTLY COMPLIANT - Dependency vulnerabilities (S1-1, S2-3, S2-4) are misconfiguration of supply chain
owasp_top_10_2021.A06_Vulnerable_Outdated_Components: HIGH PRIORITY - 8 CVEs in axios, @typescript-eslint, express, ws (see S1-1, S2-3, S2-4)
owasp_top_10_2021.A07_Authentication_Failures: GOOD - JWT + refresh rotation implemented, but weak code RNG (S0-1)
owasp_top_10_2021.A08_Software_Data_Integrity: GOOD - No auto-update mechanisms that could be hijacked; dependencies pinned in package-lock
owasp_top_10_2021.A09_Logging_Monitoring: ADEQUATE - Telemetry tracked for key events (account.deleted, etc.), but no SIEM or alerting for failed deletion attempts
owasp_top_10_2021.A10_SSRF: NOT APPLICABLE - No server-side URL fetching (Google OAuth uses client-side redirect)

sprint_immediate: Fix S0-1 and S0-2 (weak RNG) - 1.5 hours coding + testing
Create PR with changes, add CHANGELOG entry
Deploy to production and audit logs for deletion attempts
sprint_1_week: Update axios to >=1.7.7 and test OAuth flows
Run npm audit and resolve S2-2, S2-3, S2-4 vulnerabilities
Add IDOR tests for family.ts and profile.ts routes (S1-3)
sprint_2_3_weeks: Evaluate httpOnly cookie approach for token storage (S1-2 planning)
Implement CSP headers to reduce XSS attack surface
Audit all user input surfaces for XSS (event names, profiles, journal)
sprint_future: Refactor token storage to memory + httpOnly cookies (if httpOnly approach approved)
Add SIEM/alerting for suspicious auth patterns (multiple failed deletion attempts, rapid secretKey guesses)
Implement certificate pinning for API calls (defense-in-depth against MITM)

unitTests: Test that cryptographic random functions are used for all security tokens (S0-1, S0-2)
Test deletion code is not predictable (entropy >= 20 bits)
integrationTests: Test IDOR on all couple-scoped endpoints with valid JWT but wrong coupleId in body
Test deletion code brute-force protection (max 5 attempts per code)
Test refresh token rotation on multiple concurrent requests
securityTests: E2E: Attempt to delete another user's account by guessing/brute-forcing codes
E2E: Attempt couple IDOR via modified coupleId in profile update
Load test: 1000 concurrent email retries to verify no thundering herd (S2-1)

documentationRequired: SECURITY.md - Security policies and contact for disclosure
SECURITY_AUDIT_FIXES.md - Tracking of each S0/S1 fix with deployment date
THREAT_MODEL.md - Detailed threat model with attack scenarios
CONTRIBUTING.md update - No hardcoded credentials, use .env.test
ARCHITECTURE.md section on token storage and refresh flow (document localStorage decision and XSS mitigations)
